speaker: interviewer
text: --Fink in Rancho Mirage, California on February 2, 1996. What was the four years like of being in the resistance? What feelings has it left with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, the feeling of being completely abandoned-- that there wasn't any chance to be rescued by any country in the world, because there was no sign of that. We were constantly told by Churchill that he would be coming, for instance, next time. When the fall leaves would fall, they would also be coming there to save us. I mean, we finally lost completely any hope to be saved. It was becoming tighter and tighter. We were having less and less to eat. We were getting more and more fearful all the time because having deported so many people, there weren't that many left. So we always believed that they finally would come to us. And there was absolutely no way to relax or to think that things could be better. We didn't see any end. We couldn't see any end. We couldn't see. We could have no-- no idea that the Americans would ever come. And all we knew is that there-- a few Americans went to Canada. And from-- from Canada, they came in, parachuting, to France. And that's another thing we did-- we used to pick them up, when they were falling down, and bring them to-- they were brought to, or rather-- given an address-- they were given an address. And that address was given-- was changed for another address when they got to you. And they went to the next stop, and then the next stop, until they went to the last person who was sending them back. I mean, this was one of the things we knew was happening. But that wasn't helping us. I mean, they would-- they-- there wasn't-- there wasn't any way to be saved, and to think that this would finally be over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that there were four and a half terrible years of waiting, being scared, being-- being constantly on the defensive, and-- and not trusting anybody-- afraid to talk, afraid to exchange ideas, afraid to telephone. I mean, this was-- the telephone was the worst weapon. You never could use the telephone because you were taped.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, you knew very well that if you had-- if they any idea-- with a husband who had been-- who had been arrested four times, you can imagine that I wasn't-- I wasn't on the list of people who had no involvement. So that the-- all this was-- has to be thought about all the time and be careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life was-- I can't describe what life was like, because you-- you were sending a message through a messenger in order not to use the telephone. And everything-- everything was for four and a half years like this-- a complete insecurity.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not knowing what was happening, because the whole thing of the camps-- I mean, the whole-- that nobody ever heard of Auschwitz, nobody ever heard of concentration camps. All we knew was that we had to hide the young people, because they were taken for what we called-- what they called the forced-- the labor force. It was the labor force that the Germans were taking from every country they invaded, because supposedly, their army and their people were in Russia. They were on the Russian front, so they were taking all our young people. And so that was one other thing that we had to do-- we had to hide them. We had to hide them the best we could, so that they wouldn't be taken to send-- to sent in slave labor, forced labor, or whatever they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, it was constantly things like these that came our way with that. But I said, what did you mean by join? What did you mean by work?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was no such a thing. Things, as they came, had to be taken care of. If somebody was knocking at your door, and wanted to be helped and to be saved, you had to take them up in the mountain. I mean, this went-- these went out all the time. The one thing-- the one thing that you had to be so careful about-- for instance, one day, my husband was calling from a telephone booth. And he was calling a-- a very old friend of his, not knowing that it was him that he was calling, because they were always under other names. So they-- he was calling because there were very definite things to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a code language, he gave him all the instruction that had to be given. And at the end of the telephone conversation, he said, happy New Year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it just happened to be three days before Rosh Hashana. And they thought like this-- that he did that without thinking. So immediately as he walked out of the telephone booth, there was somebody there, who hooked him up immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was taken in the police station. And what-- what is it that you said? Everything else in that conversation was-- was coded and was really, very bad. That one thing hit them-- what's happy New Year in September? And he went for questioning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, they always managed-- I mean, if they-- if you weren't taken that day, you were taken the next. You managed to get out of it one time, maybe you couldn't get out of it the next time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it easy to get people to join the resistance, or be a part of the resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not easy at all. It was very slow and very hard for at least three years. At least three years. It was a very small group and it was very hard to recruit. And then the-- my husband said that a lot of people joined the last 15 minutes of the war. I mean, then we had a-- a big resisting-- Even our president, Mr. Mitterrand first worked for Vichy. When he came back from the prisoner of war camp, he did the same thing my husband did-- he also walked back. And he went to Vichy and worked for Petain. And then, in 1943, he joined the Resistance. That was very late in the game.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many more people joined late in the game?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. So many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you noticed it? You could see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, my husband especially because he had groups form that were much bigger. And we had to form new groups, i mean, and to then-- to then-- and find new places to hide them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how often would you and your husband get to see each other? Would he sometimes be able to get to the castle for a night? Or how did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: How often he was coming?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not very often. The last time I saw him was the 25th of May 1944. The day of his 35th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was at the castle?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the last time I met him, I met him in in Lyon-- in the big city of Lyon. And spent-- we had dinner together, spent the night there, he left the next morning. And I left to go back where I lived. He was killed exactly a month later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first find out that he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was awful. This friend of mine-- this friend of mine that I mentioned when I said he was the one who was playing double games, and making those ID cards, and all of that-- and he was little man who looked like nothing. I mean, my-- my second husband met him when I took him to France. And he was absolutely flabbergasted to see a little man like this, with absolutely no-- no prestige, no nothing. How could he have done so much in the underground? And I couldn't finish to explain to him that only those kind of people could work, because the ones who were very obvious, you couldn't-- you couldn't use them. And he went, by bicycle, 200 kilometers to come and tell me that he had heard that my husband had been wounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I said immediately, I'm going. Where is he? I'm going. He said, no, you're not going because it's completely in German territory. You can't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm going. If he's wounded, if he's in a hospital. I want to go. He kept saying to me-- the whole day long, he kept telling me I couldn't go. Finally, at night, he had to tell me the truth. He had been killed. And so I-- it was-- it was absolutely-- I don't know. I always felt he-- he escaped so many times for four years-- I mean, there were so many-- they were after him so many times that I didn't think it could happen again. I just didn't think so. Well, it did happen. And it happened in a very, very dramatic way because he took his whole team of men and their-- their order was to stop a train that was leaving, through Switzerland, to go back to Germany with all the files of the Resistance-- with all the files of the people who were-- that they had been able to snitch out. And the order west to stop that train at a certain spot in the-- in a mountain-- in the valley down below the railroad track, where then they were up in the mountain, themselves. And they went in the farm of these men that you saw mentioned on that same plaque where my husband was killed, because this farmer had immediately volunteered to have him put all these people from the plane in his farm. And then he would, little by little, take them to his camp in a big city, which was about 40 miles away. And they did that. They put plastic on the railroad tracks, and they bring all these-- these German up in that farm, and tons of files, and tons of papers. And my husband took whatever he could take in a big truck that he had. He didn't want any of the boys to go with him, because they weren't enough to-- to take care of the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went and unloaded that truck full, and came back empty. And when he came back empty, there was-- in that train load of people, there was one man from the Middle East. You heard, from the Middle East? The Middle East was the French Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- it was-- they were trained by the Germans and they were even worse than the Germans. And they--he denounced him. He denounced the fact that he was going to his camp and he would come-- he would come back empty. And they arrested him one kilometer before-- before his people-- before his men who were keeping those German. And they dragged him. They-- they-- they shot the four tries, so there was no way he could escape. There was nothing he could do. And they tortured him from early morning until very late at night. They never got anything out of him. And he was killed in the village. In every village in France, there is a place where the women come wash-- a wash place-- I don't know how you call that. And that's where you say that plaque that they put there, the day he was killed. And they didn't know his name. They didn't know who he was. And the peasant-- the peasant that night made a casket, and put him in a casket, and buried him. And because some people had made a casket for him, the next day, the Germans came to pick them up. And-- and-- they-- they killed three men-- they killed three men over his tombstone, because they had buried him. And so his-- his men arrested a guy in the big city where they had their camp. They arrested him, dragged him up there, and they tortured him, and they killed him on my husband's tombstone. I mean, there's been-- you don't know. This was-- this was our life. And so when I finally went there-- and I finally was able to go because the Germans finally left France-- I went there-- and I went to the woman where he had-- where all these guys used to spend the night when they went on duty. She always had rooms for them. And I was told to go and see her. She would take me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there, and the first thing she said to me, that you have to recognize him. And I said, no, I'm sorry. I won't. But she said, the religion say that you have to do it. I said, not my Jewish religion. The Jews are not-- are not bound to recognize someone who has been so tortured that I would not recognize him. I don't want that kind of memory in my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I brought a lot of pictures. I brought as many pictures I possibly could, so that they would realize who he was and that was him. I gave his name, because he was buried under unknown with a big cross on his tombstone. And I was able-- I was able to, at least, identify him. And-- and I rent a car, but I took the children first. I took the children up there. I wanted them to be there and to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took them and then I went back with them. And then, a few days later, I left them with my in-laws and I rent a car. And I took the casket in the car-- it was kind of a station wagon-- and I drove that casket in the car, in that open car, all the way to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never stopped. I never stopped for food. I never stopped to sleep anywhere. I was about 600 miles from Paris. And I was so afraid they would-- they would steal the casket. I don't know why, but it was a terrible fear. And I finally arrived in Paris. And his men were there, and they made a honor guard in a cemetery. And he was buried there, of course, and normally. And today, there is a plaque on his tombstone that-- that says who he was, and how he died for France, and-- and the most courageous and the-- whatever way that it was. And this was-- this was the end of our life-- my children and I-- when we suddenly found ourselves without him. And without anybody, because my in-laws were very tired of having had the whole family with them and done everything for them. And my parents were in America. So I was-- I was completely alone with the kids. And that's when I put the kids in the Chambon again, and I started with the children homes. And I start-- we started to build children homes after children homes to all the kids that we had kept in hiding. And we tried to get them all in Jewish homes. To--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about that in a minute because when you started working with the children, the war was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you first found out that your husband had died, the war was not over yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, immediately, I had to do something. I mean, I was responsible for my children, and I was alone, and I, immediately, had to do something. But then I was going to work, and then finally, someone said, look, there is absolutely no way out for you. You can't, you can't because the kids have to be taken back and they have to be put together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we know that their parents will never come back, and we have thousands of kids on hands. What are we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We finally ended up with 1,100 children. We made-- we had 15 homes, and we had 1,100 children. And some of them were hidden, and a lot of them came back-- some came back from Buchenwald, some came back from other camps. None of them came back from Auschwitz. Bigger ones did-- older ones did come from Auschwitz, because I had two sisters-- and one was in the east part of Auschwitz, the other one in the west. They never knew they were in camp together, and they found themselves in my home. They suddenly discovered that the two of them were alive, and they had both been in Auschwitz, and didn't know anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was it from when you heard that your husband was killed-- when the man came and told you that he was dead-- until liberation? How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. We were liberated on August 24-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he was still in German-- in German territory, so it must have been-- wait. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] It must have been about-- about three, four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about being liberated? When liberation came, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- that we fought-- for that we fought very hard. We really went out. I mean, we went out and we-- and we chased them out. And whatever we could find, we used. And it was unbelievable. It was absolutely unbelievable to see the Germans sitting at the table in the cafe in Grenoble, and the next morning, the Americans were sitting there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable. At the same cafe, the Americans were having coffee. The night-- the night before, the Germans were there. During the night, they all fled like rats. It was unbelievable-- absolutely unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were at the castle then, up in the mountains, when-- when the Americans came? That's where you were, in the little town? You were near Grenoble, when the Americans came?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Grenoble. I mean, this house we had for the 16 of us was 40 kilometers from Grenoble. And so we did the liberation of Grenoble. I mean, we-- we fought with everybody else. I mean, whoever fought, we fought with them. And they chased them out. It was unbelievable. They use snipers on the roof, and everything possible, but they still went. They still left. And they felt that they were lost. I mean, I think when they lost France, they felt defeated. In spite of the terrible war in the German front and in spite the of the loss-- terrible loss of the Americans on the German front, Hitler felt very well that it was lost. If he couldn't keep Paris for himself, it was-- that was the end of his ambition. He was sure that he would keep Paris forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and the people helped to-- to sniper and chase the Germans out? And from one night to the next, they were gone? Within one day, the Germans were out of Grenoble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, it took a little bit more in Paris, because the French Resistance in Paris fought-- lost a tremendous amount of people. A tremendous amount, because they fought them one to one. I mean, they were-- they were-- each one of them took a German and pulled him apart. And the German had snipers on all the roofs in Paris, and a lot of them-- there had been a lot of losses. It had been very tough. I went to the-- I went to the 50th anniversary of the liberation of Paris last year. And we re-did the whole thing. I mean, the whole liberation of Paris took place. They-- they-- because, It was a strange feeling for me, because I knew those young people who were there and showing how they fought, they weren't the ones, because, after all, it was 50 years before. But it was unbelievable what they did-- used the same-- the same cars that we had. They used the same bicycle. At one point, there were 200 of them on bicycles, showing what happened when we had nothing else but bicycles to fight with. It was fabulous. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you know, I think it's-- I don't know if this has already been said, but I said that there must be a record made of what happened to the French people during the war, what happened to the French Jews, and how they fought for their country-- and-- and how the patriots, and the first ones who joined the Resistance were the Jews. I mean, they didn't wait for the last 15 minutes. My husband was surrounded by Jewish friends. And this had never been told enough. It's not-- it's not published. It's not in the-- it's not in the-- in the encyclopedia. And it must be said, because-- because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the way the French Jews fought for their country and fought the Germans was really something to-- to-- to be-- not to be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war is over and you start working with the children. As soon as the war is over, you put your children in Chambon, and you start to help the orphans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't get that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the liberation, you-- you very quickly started to work with the orphans, with the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was no way out, because they were coming, coming, coming and we didn't know. We really didn't expect anything. We didn't expect any children to come back from the camps. And I remember what-- one little David that I had, who was 12 years old. And I couldn't send him to school, I couldn't make him-- one American brought a football one day, and the kids have never seen that, of course. And so they started to play-- and he wasn't playing. He wouldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only thing he knew was that he was waiting for his parents. I mean, there was nothing else in his life. And of course, the parents never came back. And it was one of the most dreadful, dreadful things for us to cope with, because we-- we had to take care of these children like if we were their parents. And trying to-- trying to make them understand. They couldn't understand, why did that happen? How could the kids understand that? The only thing is the ones who were hidden in France, and who didn't want to die because they were Jews-- that they knew. That they knew. So right after the liberation, what did you-- what did you do first? What was the first thing that you worked on?

speaker: interviewee
text: To do what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You. What did you do after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when we started the children homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started the children's' homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when we started to gather children from all over France. We sent social workers into different place where we knew we had placed children. And we brought them to Paris. I mean, our first home was in Paris. And then we had a second and a third. We had three in the surroundings of Paris. And then we had-- we had-- we had the others elsewhere. But then-- and then we had one for the-- for the little kids, and then we had one for the teenagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the teenagers, we sent back to school. And some of them finally went onto BA, and BS, and a Master, and so forth. And we were completely responsible. And it was very interesting, because I don't know if you know about ORT? But the ORT organization-- was-- I was the first one they contacted in Paris when they started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a big, big piece of land around the first house that I had. And they asked me if I would be willing to let each child have a little piece of garden, and that they would show them what it means to grow-- for the seeds to grow, and for-- and they were-- this was one of the most fabulous ways that the kids started to forget. When they were going to work in their garden, they-- they couldn't wait to go the next day to see if one flower bloomed, or if one carrot came out, or if there were some peas. And it was absolutely fantastic. I mean, I was forever grateful to ORT for that, because it was fabulous. And then also what they did in the DP camps. You know-- they had workshops in most of the DP camps. And they trained those people who were-- God knows what they were before they were deported. I mean, they-- they couldn't go back in the same-- in the same profession or whatever they were because they were going to go to Israel. And they couldn't start being a doctor, or a lawyer, or anything in Israel, not knowing the language. And they had to get a skill. And ORT was able to really give them a skill that they used when they arrived in Israel and used it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What organization were you working for, when you say we? Was it still a part of the resistance? Or what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war-- after liberation, I was involved with-- there's a European branch of Haganah, which was called B'richa. And B'richa's main-- main act was to take the people out of the camps and send them to Palestine. And so there were many organizations that were working, of course. One was Betar. And Betar was Likud. And Likud was the party of Begin. And people didn't know. They put their name down on the list. All they want to know-- was to go, leave, get out. And so they put their name down on the first guy who came and put the list in front of them-- put your name. And those who were with Betar never left. They weren't leaving. The ones with B'richa were leaving, because they were Labor. And the Labor Party, of course, took care of them. They had enough boats.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I did that once. I mean, I went up with a young man, by the name of [NON-ENGLISH] who was in Munich. And we went up-- do you remember the story of the Exodus boat? That the British took them to a German camp in Papendorf? We went to Papendorf to get some of them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so some of our guys were putting themselves in British uniforms as British MPs. And when the MPs were shifting guard, they were taking their place, and getting into the camp, and getting a-- a truck full of people, and go. And so we went. And we went through the whole of Germany-- the French zone, the British zone, and finally, the last one was the Russian zone. The American zone was the first. And when we got to the Russian zone, [NON-ENGLISH] was stopped. We had no problem anywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was stopped by the guard there, and he said, niet. So do we get papers? Niet. We got nothing. We got nothing-- no papers. And this kid had a presence-- an extraordinary presence. He picked out a paper from his pocket. That was his Israeli birth certificate, written in Hebrew. The guy you knew from nothing thought Hebrew was Russian letters. What did he know? He didn't know better.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, go. And that's the way we finally arrived in Rome. And we used, in Rome, the former Hollywood of-- of Rome, which is called Cinecitta. And we put our people there, dragged them. You can imagine how they were all DPs, and had nothing with them-- I mean, no baggage, nothing. And we had one part of the Hollywood, and the other part were actors in uniform, in costume, and everything. And you should have seen what that meant. It was unbelievable. Anyway, two nights later, they-- they I was rung at 3 o'clock in the morning. We'll pick you up. So they picked me up and we had three trucks, three buses full of people and we went about, oh, 50 kilometers from Rome on a beach. And it was the 31st of January. There was no moon. It was terribly dark. And the kids from B'richa were there, practically naked, in the water-- blowing-- blowing rubber boats that we got from the American army. And we were putting 10 or 12 in a rubber boat. And they had already, before we came, established a wire between the beach and the boat that was about two miles away in the deep sea. And they always had somebody strong enough to hold that wire all the way from the beach to the boat. And we put 10 people at a time in a rubber boat. And that's the way we were doing immigration to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh my. Let's take a break.
